name: Sokkvabek
description: Sökkvabekkr, meaning "sunken bank" or "treasury bank," is a dwelling place in Norse mythology associated with the goddess Sága and the god Odin. It is described as a hall with a roof made of glass, located in Asgard, the realm of the gods. Here, Odin and Sága are said to drink together from golden cups, sharing wisdom and knowledge. The location's name and association with drinking suggest a place of gathering, feasting, and the exchange of secrets. Sökkvabekkr represents a sanctuary of intellectual and spiritual nourishment, where the Allfather seeks counsel and solace. Its glassy roof implies transparency and clarity of vision, essential for Odin's role as a wise and far-seeing ruler. The hall's connection to Sága, whose name is linked to storytelling and history, further emphasizes its role as a repository of knowledge and tradition. The act of drinking together symbolizes intimacy and shared understanding between Odin and Sága, highlighting the importance of companionship and collaboration in the pursuit of wisdom. Some scholars believe that Sága is simply another aspect or name of Frigg, Odin's wife, thus making Sökkvabekkr a part of Odin's own dwelling. The hall remains an enigmatic location, hinting at the depths of Odin's wisdom and the importance of seeking knowledge from various sources.
subtitle: Hall of Wisdom and Shared Drinks

keyMyths.title: Odin and Sága's Counsel
keyMyths.description: Odin frequently visits Sökkvabekkr to drink with Sága and share wisdom. This place is where they exchange knowledge and secrets.

keyMyths.title: The Glass Roof
keyMyths.description: The hall's roof is made of glass, symbolizing clarity and Odin's ability to see clearly. This allows him to oversee the realms.
